item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exactly the part that bothers me. That science teacher is obviously crucial to all three SCP, and all the info we get is:

"rather, two teachers claimed he left the building but was called back in by a science teacher to pick up a report card. The science teacher in question could not be located."

No description, no further investigation, explanation, interviews with the two teachers, nothing??? Why?

And then in 1380 it says:

"Students were redirected to other schools throughout the county while Vincent Sender Elementary School was constructed in memory of a science teacher who was not seen again during (suggested edit: after) the 11/02/07 crisis. Foundation researchers believe he was lost within SCP-1380 at some point"

So no one looked into this Vincent Sender dude???

Also: all three articles could benefit greatly from some editing. Some parts are just awkward to read.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), as the image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Suggestion as the author of 3477:

Perhaps we could move the footnote back a sentence, so that it would be located after the sentence saying that all the Tyler Buchanan's are identical? Currently, it is after one of the biggest points of contention between the two articles: how they react to each other. (1680 = extreme emotional distress, 3477 = friendliness and cooperation) The new location would point out similarities after describing the most similar feature, rather than the most different.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
(Commenting for reference.)

The MCF link is good, but the -3477 link could be handled better. "Further insight […] can be gleaned" implies that reading SCP-3477 will yield answers regarding -1680, which it won't. It'd be better to say something along the lines of "possible relationship with SCP-3477 is under investigation". Also, it would fit the format of the other links much better if it wasn't a footnote, but its own paragraph.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Modified edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show edits
- Hide

To link to the mentioned GOI and a recent article with similar themes:

The Foundation had received reports suggesting that Group of Interest Lambda-33 ("Manna Charitable Foundation") was maintaining a warehouse for collecting anomalous or potentially anomalous artifacts at the former site of Mission High School.

to

The Foundation had received reports suggesting that Group of Interest Lambda-33 ("Manna Charitable Foundation") was maintaining a warehouse for collecting anomalous or potentially anomalous artifacts at the former site of Mission High School.

and

For information regarding an additional anomalous event connected to SCP-1380, please consult documentation for SCP-1380.

to
For information regarding an additional anomalous event connected to SCP-1380, please consult documentation for SCP-1380.1

Footnotes
1. Further insight into this phenomenon can be gleaned from SCP-3477, though a direct link between the two has yet to be established.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember having read the other ones long ago, this is one of those connected articles that leave you scratching your head, but I like it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Been waiting for this one since 1408 going in order. wasnt worth the wait, but also not bad. I was expecting something more special with him regarding the phenomena. no vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is "Mission High School" supposed to be in Dresden or somewhere else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Calling it. This kid's dad graduated with the Class of '76.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was…interesting. I'm honestly not sure how I feel about it. Mostly because I haven't read the other two yet, most likely.

I feel significant discomfort at the idea of using kids as D-class…but I recognize the pragmatism. The logic behind it is undeniable, and Overwatch has to make the logical decision. Granted, what Mann said upthread is true, that they could very easily have undiscovered anomalous properties. But until there's an incident, this may be the best solution. As sad as that is.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I read the article, I figured they were using the kids for labor and stuff, you know, cleaning the halls, working with safe class SCPs, restocking vending machines, etc. Somehow, I think that feeding kids to 682 wouldn't go over well with researchers.

Also, I'm surprised that the foundation ethics committee has nothing to say about this. Usually when an SCP article includes a gross violation of human rights, the writer puts in a little addendum from them about how they should terminate it, or find an alternate plan, or something.

Regardless, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr. Jones is one cold-hearted asshole. Children assigned to keter duty. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Vincent Sender
What the fuck have you done
You've sent your science lab to mexico, you've stranded a school bus on Titan that now speaks in morse, and you've taken one kid off of that bus and cloned him thousands of times.

Shit, Vince, you had ONE JOB.

Also, author, one problem: If they have over 2400 or whatever, why is the one in the picture above 3000? Was the picture added after the article was created (in universe)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a long-time science teacher, I like that part. I want to know what sort experiment he was running in the back room that caused these events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm. I like the three overall, but I had to downvote this one. I just don't like the idea of using the children as D-Class.

Not because it's wrong, but because it's dangerous. "Otherwise non-anomalous." How does the Foundation know? They could have all sorts of other anomalous properties that just haven't been observed yet. Their method of generation is not understood. They don't have any idea where these clones are coming from, or why. They could one day just start sprouting tentacles and summoning elder gods, for all the Foundation knows.

It'd be better if they just euthanized and incinerated them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the moment, it seems I was mistaken.
1580 is in fact occupied. But the current holder seems to be early in his learning curve and didn't put a title on the main list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I must have missed that. I thought it was full when I glanced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked them individually, and am intrigued by their connectivity. I keep looking for meaning in the designation numbers, but none is presenting itself.

Why no 1580? The slot is open, was it not at the time? Or is there a reason?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And everything comes full circle with the science teacher. This might be my favorite of the three; certainly, reading them in the order posted is necessary, I'd say. This is just great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly don't know what's going on with these three. I see the references to each other in each article, I've figured out the timeline and what happened when, but yeah, there's no "Oh now I get it" going on in my head. Perhaps I'm just not sharp enough to figure it out. On the other hand, I did have an idea: the part that states how all the witnesses had memory lapses at the time of the anomaly, is that just a side-effect of the anomaly, or did the GoI get in there and administer their own amnesics to them? That would interest me greatly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, I really didn't see how they could be effectively condensed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not overly fond of any of these three. None of them are giving me that "Ah, aha!" moment, and quite honestly I think they could have been condensed into a single article, because it's really just one anomaly spread out over three articles, rather than three anomalies all interconnected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am another who enjoys all three and likes the way they are connected. I'd have to say 1480 is my favorite of the bunch, and this I like least. Nonetheless, they all interest me and I give each a hearty +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see the appeal in having all three scips being a different class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *sings* D-Class Babies, they'll make your nightmares come true…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could see maybe dumping the kids with Serpent's Hand or some other group with off-dimension contacts.

I'm inclined to agree with Scantron. This reads like a weaker version of the Bonifay articles. And I'm not a big fan of those either. The Titan one is mildly interesting, but the other two, especially the science lab, are pretty bland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. That's more reasonable, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I'm not one to complain about mystery in general — most of my backstories are a Guide Dang It. Thing is, here, there's not enough information with these to support what I consider to be one very long article that, without the backstory, isn't that great.

I suppose what I'm saying is: These differ from the Bonifay artifacts (which I like) in that they are intriguing on their own, and the interconnected mystery elements enhance that. The Dresden Elementary articles, on the other hand, aren't anything to write home about on their own, so when you throw in the interconnectedness, it's not supporting anything.

Just my two cents, really. It boils down to "I don't like the base objects, so I don't care enough about them being interconnected to work at figuring it out.". Maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot help my love for interconnected shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Buhh —buhh — mystery!

Additionally, as with most things I write about the Foundation, the motives of the unreliable narrators should be taken into account. The Foundation is pretending like it simply can't piece these three things together, when instead, it's pretending to not be putting the puzzle together until it knows where everything goes.

But seriously, by most standards, isn't it acceptable to leave a few things in the hands of the reader? And look, Ma; no redactions!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea was for the three SCPs to be interconnected, related to the same originating event, and presumably related to some originating group (though the latter is NOT supposed to be seen as a GoI, hence all the mystery and vague references). Basically, one single article containing all of these events would be bugfuck long and inordinately complex, given that the Foundation can obviously see that there is a connection between them but has absolutely no means of putting it together. Moreover, the most conspicuous shared thread is the amorphous blackness, and that's not meant to be a character so much as a…side-effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, you're right. In an earlier draft, the rate at which the children were arriving was increasing at an exponential rate, basically so it could one-up the Keter Cakes (from which this drew a little inspiration). I got rid of it because I suck at making exponents do what I want them to. Euclid it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dislike the fundamental concept of having three articles to describe one phenomenon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They already solved that problem with D-Class kids. It doesn't provide an active threat to humanity or whatever as far as I understand it, and its containment isn't too difficult. I just… I don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My original post said the same thing; I don't know why this is Keter either. There is obviously a lot of difficulty in keeping this under wraps, but I don't think this fulfills the other requirement for Keter: displays an active hostility/threat to humanity. I suppose if this has the same problem as that cake Keter, it might work…..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because of the difficulty in containing 2400+ copies of a single child?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it is because I just woke up but I an not sure why keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Three scips all interconnected…this is thoroughly confusing; I'm still trying to piece everything together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like it. All three of it. Not seeing the game you're playing, mate.

E: Any of the three would have been fine on its own, or if they'd been unconnected and not obviously related they might've worked, but the attempts to connect them just feel like "LOL, A THING HAPPENED HERE AND I'M NOT TELLING YOU WHAT".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. All three of it. Well played mate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looks like someone has a thing for the number 80.

I like this one. I was taken aback by the suggestion to use innocent children as D-Class, but reading your explanation, it does seem like the most logical solution given the circumstances. Terrible, awful, but logical.

EDIT: BTW you used both 'amnesiacs' and 'amnestics' in the same paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The bus doesn't look short to me, really.
At least from the angle in the picture, that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TO ADDRESS SOME QUESTIONS I AM ANTICIPATING:

1. Dresden is a town near where I live. It has nothing to do with the Dresden Files.

2. No, I did not have the Foundation turn a bunch of kids into D-class because of teh grimdark. I put myself in a place where the Foundation was faced with an influx of something conspicuously anomalous (identical children are fairly easy to spot), plentiful, and that the Foundation could not stop the influx of. The Foundation must hide these children from view; you can't just stick a bunch of identical children in orphanages around the world when they keep coming; that solution's not practical in the long run. The way I saw it, the Foundation's only options were a)give all of the children plastic surgery and amnestic treatment and throw them into the foster system or something similarly dismissive, which would be fairly expensive and complex; b) raise all of the children to adulthood, which would be expensive, labor intensive, and would raise complex issues when the 1680 clones came to adulthood (they're still clones, so…train them as researchers? What if they don't want to? What if they freak out when they see more of themselves?) c) kill all of the children immediately, or d) use the children as D-class. The latter option keeps them hidden in Foundation facilities, gets use out of them (do you think the Foundation isn't pragmatic?), and may or may not result in their being far fewer of them over time.

I don't like the idea of the Foundation brainwashing and killing children, but life has always been fairly cheap to Overwatch, and I don't see a better option.

3. Nothing offensive is meant by the use of a short bus. I just liked that particular picture.
